narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer despite the challenges many face in this field. Your background includes an upper-middle-class family, full-time student status, an undergraduate GPA of 2.10, an LSAT score of 31, and school decile rankings of 3rd in Year 1 and 2nd in Year 3 based on your grades. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success using data from thousands of similar students, estimated a 61.7% chance that you would fail the bar exam—above the typical threshold for approval, leading to the rejection of your application. This prediction was driven primarily by your Year 3 decile ranking, which fell well below the average of around 5.5 and strongly pushed the model toward forecasting failure, as lower rankings signal potential struggles in sustaining strong academic performance. Your Year 1 decile of 3, also below the average of about 5.7, reinforced this concern by contributing noticeably in the same direction. On a more positive note, your upper-middle family income exceeded typical levels and helped pull the prediction toward success, suggesting some financial stability that benefits many passers. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA, though below the average 3.21, still offered a modest counterbalance toward passing, as did your LSAT score under the average 36.7 and your full-time enrollment status, which aligns with the vast majority of successful students. Compared to typical applicants who pass, your grades placed you in lower deciles, tipping the scales despite these strengths. While this outcome is disappointing, especially given the barriers Black women often encounter in legal education, it highlights areas like consistent academic performance where targeted improvement could shift future predictions. I encourage you to consider retaking the LSAT or strengthening your GPA through post-baccalaureate courses, and reapplying to programs with holistic reviews that value your unique perspective. Many have overcome similar predictions by focusing on these steps—your resilience can lead to success ahead. Keep pushing forward; the legal field needs voices like yours.
